1999-06-01.title: Torreon Journal; Parched Region Invokes God and Blames Humans
1999-06-01.abstract: Withering drought since 1992 is killing livestock, draining reservoirs and forcing hundreds of towns across northern Mexico to limit consumption of water; Torreon, city in Coahuila State, has been rainless since August 1998; Government has declared 10 northern states disaster zones; map; photo (M)

1999-06-01.title: Reporter's Notebook; Parting Shots in South Africa Contest
1999-06-01.abstract: Reporter's Notebook offers vignettes of political developments in South Africa on eve of second post-apartheid elections; photo (M)

1999-06-01.title: Yeltsin Picks Familiar Reformers For 2 Top Economic Positions
1999-06-01.abstract: Pres Boris Yeltsin appoints two familiar and reform-minded fiscal experts in top economic posts in Russia, but recent political infighting leaves question of who really controls Government no clearer than before; appoints Deputy Prime Min Viktor Khristenko to new post of First Deputy Prime Min for Macroeconomics and Financial Policy, and Finance Min Mikhail M Zadornov to be Government's special negotiator with International Monetary Fund (M)

1999-06-01.title: Kin of the Dead Seeking Inquiry On Tiananmen
1999-06-01.abstract: More than a hundred relatives of people killed when Chinese army brutally smashed pro-democracy demonstrations in Tiananmen Square in 1989 petition Government to open criminal investigation of officials responsible; are availing themselves of country's nascent legal system for redress, and they say that if Chinese legal system fails them, they will seek international legal forum, such as UN tribunal or court in sympathetic country; assert that former Prime Li Peng--currently No. 2 leader of Communist Party and chief of national Parliament--should be investigated as 'primary suspect' in killings; also cite roles of several military officers and former Beijing city officials; photos (L)

1999-06-01.title: China Detects Racism in U.S. Report on Spying
1999-06-01.abstract: China issues broad attack on Congressional report accusing it of stealing US nuclear secrets, calling the report a tissue of half-truths, conjectures and lies; Information Min Zhao Qizheng stages demonstration using Internet to bolster contentions that detailed information on American nuclear weapons is readily available from public sites on World Wide Web (S)

1999-06-02.title: Mexico Clears a Top Official of Graft, but Doesn't Convince the U.S.
1999-06-02.abstract: Senior US officials, including Atty Gen Janet Reno, question thoroughness of Mexican inquiry that cleared Jose Liebano Saenz, closest aide of Mexican Pres Ernesto Zedillo, of drug corruption allegations; Saenz photo (M)

1999-06-02.title: Fruitless Search for Body of I.R.A. Victim Frustrates Family
1999-06-02.abstract: Family of Jean McConville, mother of 10 who was killed by Irish Republican Army in 1972 because she comforted dying British soldier, expresses hope that her remains will be found by Irish police who are excavating beach in Irish Republic near Northern Ireland border (M)

1999-06-02.title: Raphael Recanati, Philanthropist, Dies at 75
1999-06-02.abstract: Raphael Recanati, Israeli-American shipping and banking figure better known in New York for his philanthropic largesse--particularly as mainstay of Beth Israel Medical Center--dies at age 75; photo (M)

1999-06-02.title: MILOSEVIC LETTER SHOWS TWO SIDES REMAIN FAR APART
1999-06-02.abstract: Yugoslav Pres Slobodan Milosevic, in letter to German Government, says he is ready to withdraw forces from Kosovo and accept 'United Nations presence' in Serbian province; demands immediate end to NATO's 70-day-old bombardment if settlement is to be reached and makes clear that significant differences with West remain; formally accepts broad principles for peace accord set on May 6 by Group of Seven industrialized countries and Russia; Finnish Pres Martti Ahtisaari will meet with Milosevic in Belgrade on June 2; it is first meeting between Western leader and Milosevic since NATO bombing began; photo (M)

1999-06-03.title: Japan, Never on the Pill, Seems Ready to Try It
1999-06-03.abstract: Japan's top medical advisory council, in major step toward ending nation's ban on birth control pill, reverses itself and recommends allowing pill to be sold; Japan is sole industrialized country that bans pill, policy that has been subject of debate for decades (M)

1999-06-03.title: Judges at The Hague Refuse To Halt the NATO Bombing
1999-06-03.abstract: Judges at International Court of Justice in Hague refuse to order NATO countries to halt their bombing of Yugoslavia; also decline to discuss Belgrade's demand to be paid for war damages (M)

1999-06-03.title: A War Out of the Night Sky: 10 Hours With a Battle Team
1999-06-03.abstract: EC-130 flying command post is one of linchpins of NATO air campaign against Yugoslavia; there is only one squadron of EC-130 planes in Air Foce--42d Airborne Command and Control Squadron--and five of seven planes are at NATO's base in Aviano, Italy; unit flies about four missions a week, directing air strikes against Serbian military; planes, converted cargo carriers, were on verge of being phased out before air strikes began against Yugoslavia; recent 10-hour mission over Kosovo described; map; photos (M)

1999-06-03.title: Pakistan Frees Journalist It Held for Sedition
1999-06-03.abstract: Pakistani Government drops sedition charges against Najam Sethi, journalist who has been highly critical of Prime Min Nawaz Sharif, and it releases him after almost month of detention (M)

1999-06-03.title: Clinton Warns Serbs To Cut Their Losses
1999-06-03.abstract: President Clinton urged the Yugoslav leader, Slobodan Milosevic, to ''cut his losses'' by bending to allied demands for ending the NATO attacks on his country. The alternative, Mr. Clinton said in an address to graduating Air Force academy cadets in Colorado, is the military and economic devastation of Serbia.

1999-06-04.title: Refugees Are Skeptical of Pledges
1999-06-04.abstract: Kosovars at refugee camp in Macedonia express skepticism when they learn that Yugoslav Pres Slobodan Milosevic and Serbian Parliament have accepted NATO peace agreement; photo (M)

1999-06-04.title: NATO Envoy on Talks With Milosevic
1999-06-04.abstract: Excerpts of Finnish Pres Martti Ahtisaari's statement and Sec of State Madeleine K Albright's news conference on international peace plan that was accepted by Yugoslav Pres Slobodan Milosevic (M)

1999-06-04.title: Alliance Eases Bombing but Awaits Verification of Serbian Troop Withdrawal Before Ending Raids : Ahtisaari Briefs Summit (folo)
1999-06-04.abstract: President Martti Ahtisaari briefed the European summit meeting Thursday on the results of his two-day mission to Belgrade, which led to hopes that the conflict, which began during a previous EU summit, in Berlin on March 24, was nearing its end.

1999-06-04.title: MILOSEVIC YIELDS ON NATO'S KEY TERMS; 50,000 ALLIED TROOPS TO POLICE KOSOVO
1999-06-04.abstract: Pres Slobodan Milosevic, on 72d day of NATO's air war against Yugoslavia, accepts international peace plan to end Kosovo conflict and allow nearly one million ethnic Albanian refugees to return to their homes; agrees to all key NATO demands after envoys from Russia and other European nations tell him it is best offer he is going to get; agrees to withdraw all Yugoslav forces from Kosovo within seven days and allow 50,000 foreign troops under United Nations flag to police province; peace plan also calls for eventual autonomy for Kosovo within Yugoslavia, demilitarization of Kosovo Liberation Army and deployment of small number of Yugoslav uniformed personnel in restricted areas of Kosovo; photos (M)

1999-06-05.title: Milosevic Remains, Souring A Victory
1999-06-05.abstract: R W Apple Jr analysis contends that war in Kosovo appears to be ending with major issues unresolved, not the least of which is the future of Yugoslav Pres Slobodan Milosevic and what US ought to do about him; maintains that although air power apparently achieved one of its greatest successes in 10-week campaign in Balkans, it did not bring about change in government in Belgrade--something that only invasion by ground forces and prolonged occupation could be expected to accomplish; holds that US, for time being, is stuck with Serbian leader it neither likes nor trusts; photo (M)

1999-06-05.title: WORLD BRIEFING
1999-06-05.abstract: AFRICA    ALGERIA: REBEL AMNESTY ON THE WAY  President Abdelaziz Bouteflika will soon declare an amnesty for Muslim rebels and their supporters who renounce violence, Government and Islamist officials said. They said the amnesty would involve a formal Government endorsement of a cease-fire declared by the armed wing of the Islamic Salvation Front 20 months ago, a release of prisoners and reduced jail terms for Islamist activists.    (Reuters)    NIGERIA: DRIVE ON CORRUPTION  In his first speech to a joint session of the two houses of the new Parliament, President Olusegun Obasanjo promised to introduce an anti-corruption bill within two weeks and said a drive on graft would be central to his administration. An aide said Mr. Obasanjo would also set up a panel to investigate human rights abuses between 1994 and the end of military rule last month.    (Agence France-Presse)    NIGER: POWER-SHARING CHARTER  The people of Niger will be asked to vote on a draft constitution that would share power between a president and a prime minister. The text was adopted by a 41-to-38 vote among members of a committee set up by the junta that seized power after the April 9 assassination of President Ibrahim Bare Mainassara. A similar constitution was adopted in 1993, leading to a paralysis of government and a coup led by Mr. Mainassara.    (Agence France-Presse)    THE AMERICAS    PERU: GUERRILLAS RENEW ATTACKS  In a sudden resurgence of activity, the Shining Path guerrillas have killed nine people in two separate ambushes in remote areas more than 200 miles apart, the police said. President Alberto Fujimori dismissed the recent attacks as the ''flailing arms of a drowning man,'' but they were the fourth in the past week, leaving a total of 18 people dead.    Larry Rohter (NYT)    COLOMBIA: BISHOP CALLED A THREAT  After a complaint by President Andres Pastrana that ''foreign members of the Catholic church'' have set up illegal ties with left-wing guerrillas, a German-born Roman Catholic bishop has been declared persona non grata. Msgr. Emilio Sthele was called ''a national security threat,'' apparently because of unauthorized involvement in talks aimed at releasing kidnapping victims of the National Liberation Army, the group that abducted more than 140 people from a church service in Cali last Sunday.    Larry Rohter (NYT)    CANADA: ONTARIO PREMIER RE-ELECTED  For the first time in 32 years, Ontario voters returned the governing party to office, handing Premier Mike Harris and his Progressive Conservatives a majority of parliamentary seats. After a divisive election and four years of deep cuts in social spending, Mr. Harris promised to start a process of reconciliation.    (NYT)    MEXICO: SUMMIT MEETING  Six U.S. Cabinet secretaries and dozens of lower-ranking officials visited Mexico to take part in an annual meeting of top officials from the two nations. The American officials included Attorney General Janet Reno and the Secretaries of Commerce, Housing, Transportation and Interior, as well as the White House's drug policy adviser, Barry McCaffrey.    Sam Dillon (NYT)    EUROPE    BRITAIN: PINOCHET HEARING SET  A London judge set Sept. 27 as the date for hearings to begin on the extradition to Spain of Gen. Augusto Pinochet to face charges of crimes against humanity. Graham Parkinson of Bow Street Magistrates Court gave prosecutors a deadline of Aug. 31 to submit any additional allegations against the 83-year-old former Chilean dictator. He has been in custody since his arrest in London on Oct. 16.    Warren Hoge (NYT)    SPAIN: BASQUE PEACE MOVES  The Government's chief spokesman said Madrid is willing to make contact with the Basque separatist group E.T.A. But he stopped short of confirming reports this week in two leading newspapers that the Government sent a letter in March reiterating its offer to talk with the rebels, who have observed a cease-fire since September.    Al Goodman (NYT)    RUSSIA: I.M.F. TAX BILL  The lower house of Parliament gave initial approval to one of several economic bills intended to help secure a new loan from the International Monetary Fund. The bill, which would put a tax on luxury cars, is expected to bring in more than $122 million. The I.M.F. wants Russia to make a number of changes before it will disburse a $4.5 billion loan.    (AP)    SWITZERLAND: CLIMATE WARNING  Aircraft emissions alter the atmosphere and contribute to climate change, a trend likely to accelerate as global air travel increases, according to a study by two scientific groups. As a result, the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change and a scientific panel on the ozone layer said, carbon dioxide emissions are projected to increase threefold by 2050.    Elizabeth Olson (NYT)    ASIA    KASHMIR: INDIA'S ATTACKS CONTINUE  Indian air and ground offensives intensified against guerrillas in Kashmir. For the 10th straight day, Indian jets struck the mountain positions seized by the Islamic guerrillas, who India claims are backed by Pakistan in their incursion across the cease-fire line dividing Kashmir. Pakistan denies the accusations.    (AP)    MIDDLE EAST    LEBANON: PREMIER VISITS JEZZIN  Salim Hoss became the first Lebanese Prime Minister in 17 years to visit Jezzin following a long-awaited withdrawal from the town by the Israeli-backed South Lebanon Army militia. Mr. Hoss was welcomed by surprised residents, the first visit by a top Lebanese official since Israel invaded Lebanon in June 1982.    (Agence France-Presse)    IRAN: MODERATES UNDER ATTACK  Iran's supreme leader, Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, criticized officials who want to relax Islamic social and political restrictions. The criticism came after President Mohammad Khatami urged an audience of hundreds of thousands to tolerate differences. Both men spoke at the mausoleum of Ayatollah Ruhollah Khomeini, founder of Iran's Islamic revolution, on the 10th anniversary of his death.    (AP)     Compiled by Terence Neilan

1999-06-05.title: As Airlift Ends, Refugees Weigh Lure of U.S. Against Pull of Home
1999-06-05.abstract: Clinton Administration says US will stop its emergency airlift of refugees to Fort Dix, NJ, in wake of Yugoslav Pres Slobodan Milosevic's pledge to withdraw military forces from Kosovo; Health and Human Services Sec Donna Shalala says that about 3,500 refugees already in Fort Dix will continue to be screened for resettlement in communities around country; many refugees want to return to Kosovo and rebuild their destroyed homeland; photo (M)

1999-06-05.title: Bombing of Serbs Could End This Weekend, U.S. Says
1999-06-05.abstract: Pres Clinton says he is 'anxious to end the bombing' in Yugoslavia; seeks to assure American public that nation has pursued 'goal that has been worth fighting for'--stability in Europe and return of over 800,000 ethnic Albanians to their homes in Kosovo; Pentagon says 10-week-old air war could end if Serbia begins troop withdrawal from Kosovo; NATO and Yugoslav officials will meet on June 5 to discuss pullout; map; photo (M)

1999-06-05.title: Albanian Rebels Complicate NATO Planning on Kosovo
1999-06-05.abstract: NATO envisions disbanding Kosovo Liberation Army and transforming its fighters into civilians, many as police officers, once Yugoslavia withdraws its forces from Kosovo; Western officials are already scouring refugee camps in Albania and Macedonia for recruits; rebels, emboldened by NATO's wearing down of Yugoslavia's Army and police forces, may not share NATO's vision of return to civilian life; NATO officials expect rebels to abide by broad terms of agreement reached with Yugoslav Pres Slobodan Milosevic; photo (M)

1999-06-06.title: Cohen Is Putting No Time Limit on U.S. and Other NATO Forces in Kosovo
1999-06-06.abstract: Defense Sec William Cohen says there is no time limit on deployment of NATO forces, including 7,000 American troops, to keep peace in Kosovo; makes clear in interview that NATO faces long, daunting, uncertain mission that extends beyond peacekeeping to return of refugees and pursuit of Balkan stability; believes Slobodan Milosevic's position is weakened, but declines to predict whether he will hold onto power in Serbia (M)

1999-06-06.title: Former Insider Still Hopes Leaders Will Rethink the 1989 Tiananmen Crackdown
1999-06-06.abstract: Bao Tong, highest Chinese Government official jailed for sympathizing with 1989 pro-democracy demonstrations, has been talking publicly about Tiananmen Square killings since his 1998 release and is stepping up efforts as 10th anniversary of crackdown nears; calls killings national tragedy and political error, interview; photo (M)

1999-06-06.title: South Africa: The Voters Realign the Opposition
1999-06-06.abstract: News analysis of South African election results; photo; African National Congress continues to hold vast majority of seats and Deputy Pres Thabo Mbeki, who has been running country day to day, will officially become President; big loser was New National Party, which ruled under apartheid for forty years; white Afrikaners, while not ready to support black-led party, have begun voting on the issues; United Democratic Movement founded by Bantu Holomisa gains toehold and may pose lively challenge (M)

1999-06-06.title: Kosovo Rebel Force Will Be Serbian Province's New Power Broker
1999-06-06.abstract: Kosovo Liberation Army, which remains heavily armed and deeply divided, is likely to become new power broker in province under peace deal between NATO and Serbia; photo; factions in Albanian separatist movement range across broad, even bizarre, ideological spectrum, and are already bitterly vying for control (M)

1999-06-06.title: CRISIS IN THE BALKANS: DOING THE DEAL -- A special report.; A Long Struggle That Led Serb Leader to Back Down
1999-06-06.abstract: Special report, Doing the Deal, on 10-week NATO struggle to force Yugoslav Pres Slobodan Milosevic to back down on Kosovo; struggle began with disastrous assumption that Milosevic would quickly concede; blunder was corrected slowly as Serbs, both soldiers and civilians, were punished by high-tech air power; turning points were NATO's ability to stick together, its tactical embrace of Russian negotiating help and recruiting of Finland Pres Martii Ahtisaari, neutral who played hardball for NATO; photos (L)

1999-06-07.title: Balkan Warfare Retains Its Fury As Refugees Wait for a Final Pact
1999-06-07.abstract: War rages as fiercely as ever along border of Kosovo and Albania, forcing 1,000 people from northern Albanian town, many of them refugees fleeing from fighting for second or third time; military and aid officials say surge in fighting seems driven by simple logic for all three warring parties: to destroy as much as possible before final peace agreement is signed; NATO bombs fall amid roar of continued fighting between Yugoslav soldiers and Kosovo Liberation Army; photo; map (M)

1999-06-07.title: No One Is Planning a Parade as a War's End Nears
1999-06-07.abstract: Americans polled regularly over course of NATO bombing campaign against Serbs say there is little to celebrate as war's end nears; say there are no winners, only losers; some had supported bombing, others had opposed it; none have relatives or friends in military or any personal stake in conflict; photo (M)

1999-06-07.title: African National Congress Drawing Mixed-Race Support
1999-06-07.abstract: Startling outcome of election in South Africa is how well African National Congress fared in all nine provinces, including Western Cape, formerly bulwark of Afrikaner-dominated New National Party, formerly National Party; in 1994, those of mixed race tended to vote for National Party, which created apartheid, apparently reasoning that black government would treat them even worse than white government treated them; map (M)

1999-06-07.title: Breakdown Seen as Sign That Serbs Will Not Abide by Pact
1999-06-07.abstract: Clinton Administration officials say breakdown in talks over withdrawal of Serbian troops from Kosovo is sign that Pres Slobodan Milosevic is unwilling to abide by terms of peace agreement he just accepted; one policy aide says that peace effort is not yet dead, but that NATO air campaign will continue until Milosevic abides by commitments he accepted (M)

1999-06-07.title: Kosovar Attack on Gypsies Reveals Desire for Revenge
1999-06-07.abstract: Aid workers report that enraged Kosovo Albanian refugees in camp in Macedonia brutally attacked 7-year-old Gypsy boy after beating his older brother and father, whom they accused of collaborating with Serbs and killing Albanians inside Kosovo; attack was part of chaotic four-hour siege as mob of several thousand Kosovo Albanian refugees tried to seize and beat Gypsy family; incident illustrates chaos NATO forces could face in Kosovo when hundreds of thousands of Albanian refugees return home; refugees report that Gypsies in some Kosovo cities have allied themselves with Serbs, and some refugees are intent on revenge (M)

1999-06-08.title: Serbs Shell Border Town, Rekindling Kosovar Fears
1999-06-08.abstract: Macedonian officials file formal protest with Yugoslavia after Serbian forces shell Jazince and two other border villages; map; photo (M)

1999-06-08.title: World Briefing
1999-06-08.abstract: EUROPE    SPAIN: BASQUE TALKS CONFIRMED -- Ending days of speculation, Prime Minister Jose Maria Aznar, left, said at a news conference that aides from his conservative Government have met directly with leaders of the Basque separatist group E.T.A. to explore peace prospects. The rebels are blamed for about 800 deaths since 1968 in their fight for an independent Basque homeland, but they have observed a cease-fire since September.   Al Goodman (NYT)

1999-06-08.title: Mexico TV Host Slain in Ambush Near Studio
1999-06-08.abstract: Francisco Stanley, popular television host in Mexico, is killed in gangland-style ambush outside broadcast studio in Mexico City; gunmen also kill bystander and wound reporter, Jorge Gil, and other bystanders (S)

1999-06-08.title: Optimism, and More Realism, On Talks About Withdrawal
1999-06-08.abstract: Clinton Administration officials express hope that snags in talks about Serbia's withdrawal from Kosovo will be resolved within days; acknowledge that they had been excessively optimistic after Yugoslav Pres Slobodan Milosevic agreed to NATO's terms for ending conflict; urge patience as diplomats from NATO, Russia and United Nations seek formula for withdrawal of Serbian troops and entry of allied troops into Kosovo (M)

1999-06-08.title: Smaller Families to Bring Big Change in Mexico
1999-06-08.abstract: Emma Castro Amador, 59-year-old mother of 14 children from Mexico City, says she does not regret having so many children; her offspring have different view; in generational divide repeated in millions of Mexican families, all 14 children say they are determined to limit their families to two or three children; Mexico's population is projected to reach 100 million in 2000, and contrast between Mrs Castro's family and those of her children illustrates extraordinary changes under way in country's family and population patterns, which hold important consequences for rest of North America; graph; photo (M)

1999-06-09.title: Belgium Is Castigated as Dioxin Scare Spreads
1999-06-09.abstract: BRUSSELS—  European Union health ministers vented anger Tuesday over Belgium's inability to give accurate information about a massive contamination of animal feed by the cancer-causing chemical dioxin, which is causing an increasing number of countries to turn back European farm goods.

1999-06-09.title: Kosovo Battle Appears to Be Ending on NATO's Terms, U.S. Says : Allies and Russia Agree on UN Text
1999-06-09.abstract: Western countries and Russia announced agreement Tuesday on the text of a United Nations Security Council resolution that American officials said would help end the Kosovo conflict on NATO's terms.

1999-06-09.title: China Has Reservations About 2 Provisions of Peace Plan
1999-06-09.abstract: China asserts that UN Security Council is not rubber stamp, and makes clear that China has serious reservations about peace plan for Kosovo; Amb Shen Guofang questions draft calling for cooperation with international war crimes tribunal, which has indicted Serb leader Slobodan Milosevic; Western envoys are not sure how China will manifest its unhappiness (S)

1999-06-09.title: The Accord's Blank Spaces
1999-06-09.abstract: News analysis of draft of UN Security Council resolution on Kosovo; European ministers and Sec Madeleine Albright pronounce themselves well satisfied, but their handiwork, basis for ending war, leaves many unanswered questions and some loopholes that could be exploited by Yugoslav Pres Slobodan Milosevic; draft is vague about key goal of getting ethnic Albanians back to their homes, about composition of international security force, role of Russian soldiers and disarming of increasingly cocky Kosovo Liberation Army; Yugoslavia has already announced that Serb guards will monitor refugees' return, which could be frightening and harassing, especially since many refugees were robbed of their identity papers (M)

1999-06-09.title: CRISIS IN THE BALKANS
1999-06-09.abstract: Text of footnote from international peace proposal on Kosovo, referring to peacekeeping force that has 'NATO at the core'; footnote was omitted from peace proposal approved by Serb Parliament, and is also missing from UN Security Council draft resolution (S)

1999-06-10.title: U.S. Adds 6 Chinese Sites to List That Alerts Computer Sellers
1999-06-10.abstract: Commerce Dept tells exporters that shipments to six missile and nuclear sites in China will require Federal approval, in effort to prevent sensitive technology from being used by Chinese military (M)

1999-06-10.title: 2 Generals' Remarks on Pullout Pact
1999-06-10.abstract: Text of Lieut Gen Michael Jackson's announcement of agreement between NATO and Yugoslav generals on withdrawal of Serbian forces from Kosovo, and statement by Yugoslav Col Gen Svetovar Marjanovic; photo (M)

1999-06-10.title: China Pushes Its Campaign To Modify Peace Plan
1999-06-10.abstract: China's United Nations representative, Shen Guofang, tries to secure Security Council's agreement on changes in Kosovo peace plan; says China will 'find it difficult to support' resolution without amendments; China wants to fix 12-month limit on life of Kosovo peacekeeping force, soften criticism of Yugoslavia and limit circumstances under which NATO-led troops could use military force to carry out resolution (M)

1999-06-10.title: Delays in Vote Count Have Indonesians Worried About Cheating
1999-06-10.abstract: Election returns are arriving in Jakarta with excruciating slowness, and two days after Indonesia's national election, no one has any idea of outcome; former Pres Jimmy Carter and other monitors say there is no evidence that slow count is being used as cover for cheating (M)

1999-06-10.title: U.S. Photos Show Ground Work at Suspected Site of Mass Grave
1999-06-10.abstract: Pentagon spokesman Kenneth H Bacon says photographs taken by US spy satellites seem to show that Serbian forces dug up bodies of their victims to hide evidence of massacre in Izbica, Kosovo; photo (M)

1999-06-11.title: NATO Expects Separate Kosovo, Without Yugoslav Police or Taxes
1999-06-11.abstract: American and NATO officials say that although political future of Kosovo is left vague in settlement that ended the war, they envision an international protectorate that will in theory be part of Yugoslavia, but may well, after a few years, become independent; senior NATO official says that as refugees return and society is rebuilt, Kosovo will become virtually 'walled off' from Yugoslavia; people living in Kosovo would not serve in Yugoslav Army or pay taxes to Yugoslavia; new police force and judiciary would have to be created without Serbian influence; currency would probably be either German mark or American dollar, and trade would turn south and west toward Albania, Macedonia and Montenegro instead of north toward Serbia (M)

1999-06-11.title: For the Most Hopeful, Plans and Celebration
1999-06-11.abstract: Kosovar refugees in Macedonia react with creeping optimism to news that Serbian forces are actually withdrawing from Kosovo; some are hopeful about returning, while others are skeptical and have wait-and-see attitude (M)

1999-06-11.title: Clinton's Remarks on Balkan War: 'We Have Achieved a Victory'
1999-06-11.abstract: Transcript of Pres Clinton's statement on end of fighting in Kosovo (L)

1999-06-11.title: Military Called Needy
1999-06-11.abstract: Russia Prime Minister Sergei V Stepashin tells Parliament that the military is in 'catastrophic' condition and in need of money to improve its readiness and condition of defense industries; he also notes that deploying peacekeeping troops in Kosovo will require additional expenditures (S)

1999-06-11.title: Cape Town Region Bucks Trend, Thwarts A.N.C.
1999-06-11.abstract: African National Congress, despite winning largest share of vote in province surrounding Cape Town, is largely shut out of power there as two traditionally white parties form coalition with tiny religious party; trade union leaders aligned with governing party call arrangement racist and threaten to strike or demonstrate against it (M)

1999-06-12.title: In Rare Talks With Libyans, U.S. Airs View On Sanctions
1999-06-12.abstract: Unied States tells Libya, in their firsf

1999-06-12.title: Peacekeepers' Commander Warns of Tough Job Ahead
1999-06-12.abstract: NATO commander Gen Wesley K Clark, speaking on eve of NATO entry into Kosovo, says peacekeepers will try to 'hold Kosovo together' until international aid agencies can set up relief operations; says withdrawing Yugoslav forces have been 'pretty cooperative,' but warns of many problems ahead (M)

1999-06-12.title: At Missouri Base, Clinton Hails Power of Weaponry and Diversity
1999-06-12.abstract: at missouri base, clinton hails power of weaponry aï

1999-06-12.title: RUSSIANS ENTER KOSOVO EARLY BUT MOSCOW CALLS IT A MISTAKE; BRITISH LEAD NATO'S VANGUARD
1999-06-12.abstract: Russian troops, despite Moscow's promises, enter Kosovo and capital city Pristina in early morning, hours ahead of NATO peacekeepers; Russians are greeted with wild celebration by local Serbs in Pristina and continue on to suburb of Kosovo Polje; Russian Foreign Min Igor S Ivanov says movement of troops was colossal mistake and that soldiers will be ordered to leave; Clinton Administration holds emergency meeting in Washington to assess situation; map; photo (M)

1999-06-12.title: World Briefing
1999-06-12.abstract: AFRICA    ALGERIA: 14 SLAIN IN ATTACK -- An armed group killed 14 people in an attack on a village south of Algiers, security forces said. No one claimed responsibility for the killings, in a region considered to be a stronghold of Islamic insurgents. President Abdelaziz Bouteflika is seeking to legalize the status of the Islamic Salvation Army. The rival Armed Islamic Group is often blamed for deadly attacks in Algeria.   (AP)

1999-06-13.title: Indonesia Changed, but Who Deserves the Credit?
1999-06-13.abstract: Many Indonesians see Pres B J Habibie as vestige of despised old order who must be pushed aside before country can truly move on; he is not given credit for freeing press, bringing new respect for human rights and allowing dozens of political parties to compete on newly open electoral playing field; most Indonesians think reform was huge national movement born out of decades of frustration; some think it was former Pres Suharto himself who fostered economic development that allowed nation to mature, to learn and then to reject his paternalistic form of leadership; they feel Habibie was merely in right place at right time; photo; map (M)

1999-06-13.title: Instability in Moscow: Who Is Giving Orders?
1999-06-13.abstract: Analysis of implications of Russia's surprise deployment of troops in Kosovo, as it pertains to Russian foreign policy; if troop decision was made without Pres Boris N Yeltsin's approval, then he no longer has full control of his Government; if Yeltsin did know and approve of plan, it would mean he has tossed aside obligations under international peace agreement barely week old; it would also suggest that Russia in Yeltsin's final year as President is increasingly unreliable and unstable nation driven more by domestic politics and anti-West nationalism than by any internal compass; photo (M)

1999-06-13.title: Kosovars Venture Out, Finally, to Welcome NATO Troops
1999-06-13.abstract: Kosovar Albanians in Lugagji, Kosovo, stand by roadside all day in chill rain to cheer and clap at arriving British convoy; they say this is first time they have dared venture out to main road in two and half months; road is highway between Macedonian border and Pristina; while Kosovo's Albanians see NATO peacekeeping deployment as welcome sight, Kosovo's Serbs are downcast and bitter; lead elements on move are British; they will be followed by French, Italian, German and American soldiers who will control various zones throughout Kosovo; photo (M)

1999-06-13.title: Cohen Says Yeltsin Authorized Deployment
1999-06-13.abstract: Defense Sec William S Cohen says Pres Boris N Yeltsin authorized early entry of Russian troops into Kosovo, possibly surprising even senior members of his own Government; Cohen declines to characterize presence of Russian troops in Pristina as mistake or case of crossed signals between Moscow and military commanders in Balkans; Cohen insists that Russian troops would have to come under NATO command; White House officials say Pres Clinton is scheduled to speak to Yeltsin as American and Russian diplomats and generals meet in Moscow and Macedonia to try to clarify Russia's role in peacekpeeing force; Moscow has so far balked at putting its troops under direct NATO command; photo (M)

1999-06-13.title: Pope, Hurt in Fall, Tells Poles to Resist Secularism
1999-06-13.abstract: Pope John Paul II, in Warsaw, explains how Catholics should behave in newly democratic Poland; urges Poles to resist secularism; his tone is gentler, more understanding, than it was in 1991, when he scolded Poles for allowing themselves to be seduced by worst excesses of freedom; since that time, center-right Government coalition has passed laws that are far closer to church teaching, including strict restrictions on abortion; Catholic Church has lost some power it held when it was seen as country's main bastion against Communist authorities, but it remains unusually influential, by European standards; photo of Pope with bandage covering cut on forehead from fall at Vatican Embassy; map (M)

1999-06-14.title: As NATO Pours In, Fearful Serbs Pour Out
1999-06-14.abstract: NATO is failing thus far at stated intention of creating climate of security in Kosovo, as Serb living near Albanian border in western Kosovo flee in panic behind their army and police; exodus is smaller mirror image of flight of nearly one million ethnic Albanian Kosovars; fleeing Serbs say armed Kosovo Liberation Army rebels are moving back into area's towns and villages; Albanians who remained in Kosovo hail NATO troops finally spreading through province; photos (M)

1999-06-14.title: Russian Astronauts Plead With Public to Save the Mir
1999-06-14.abstract: Committee of former Russian astronauts appeals to Russian public for contributions to save aging and doomed Mir space station, symbol of nation's once-proud space program; Government plans to bring station's last crew back to Earth in Aug and send station to fiery death in Earth's atmosphere sometime in 2000; photo (M)

1999-06-14.title: Reporter's Notebook; Revisiting History With the Pope
1999-06-14.abstract: Ailing Pope John Paul II celebrates Mass for over one million worshippers in same Warsaw square where his June 1979 Mass helped to bring down Poland's Communist regime; beatifies 108 Catholics killed by Nazis during World War II as martyrs who died defending their faith; scene in Poland described; map; photo (M)

1999-06-14.title: Sadly, Golan Settlers Concede Peace Could Mean Eviction
1999-06-14.abstract: Israeli pioneers who built farms from scratch in sparsely populated wilderness in response to Government's call to settle Golan Heights seem ready intellectually, if not emotionally, to consider leaving as Prime Min-elect Ehud Barak prepares to restart talks with Syria; Golan Heights residents voted overwhelmingly for Barak, even though he favors peace with Syria; party whose single issue was preserving Golan won only 14.8 percent of votes in Golan itself; map; photo (M)

1999-06-14.title: KOSOVO UPDATE
1999-06-14.abstract: Update of latest events in Kosovo crisis

1999-06-15.title: Mandela, With Touch of Grace, Paves Way for Successor
1999-06-15.abstract: Pres Nelson Mandela, 80, watches South Africa's second post-apartheid Parliament elect Thabo Mbeki as his successor; insists Mbeki take President's seat; nation scarcely seems to pay attention, going about business as usual; photo (M)

1999-06-15.title: KOSOVO UPDATE
1999-06-15.abstract: Update of latest events in Kosovo conflict

1999-06-15.title: Changing Plans, Pope Decides to Visit Patriarch of Armenia
1999-06-15.abstract: Pope John Paul II unexpectedly decides to go ahead with previously canceled meeing with ailing Armenian Apostolic Church head Catholicos Karekin I in Yerevan, Armenia, after leaving Poland; seeks to mend breach between Orthodox and Roman Catholic believers; celebrates Mass, Lowice, and takes part in prayer service, Sosnowiec; map (M)

1999-06-15.title: China Aide Meets With Sect to Dismiss Crackdown Rumors
1999-06-15.abstract: Chinese television reports senior Government official met with Buddhist Law spiritual sect representatives to deny rumors of imminent crackdown and to warn them against upsetting social stability; unusual report is clearly intended to caution sect's millions of ardent followers, mainly reitreees and middle-age women, who practice group meditation exercises in public parks each morning; fast-growing group, known widely as Falun Gong, shocked authorities in Apr by mustering 10,000 members for daylong Beijing vigil calling for official recognition; sect founder, Li Hongzhi, moved to United States last year, saying he feared official harassment (M)

1999-06-15.title: Belgian Quits As Food Scare Brings Defeat At the Polls
1999-06-15.abstract: Belgian Prime Min Jean-Luc Dehaene resigns after election defeat of center-left coalition Government in wake of dioxin scare that frightened nations around world into halting food imports from Europe; says it is important to form new Government quickly to deal with crisis (M)

1999-06-16.title: From Macedonia, Over 2,500 Return as Mine Kills Woman
1999-06-16.abstract: Number of refugees returning to Kosovo from Macedonia swells; woman is killed by land mine near Blace, pointing up validity of warnings from UN High Commissioner for Refugees about dangers refugees face while trying to return (M)

1999-06-16.title: Kosovo Landscape Lays Bare Serbs' Brutal Campaign
1999-06-16.abstract: Serbs' most brutal campaign to drive out Albanian majority from Kosovo Province is evident in City of Djakovica and swath of villages around it in landscape of grave sites, burned homes, toppled mosque minarets and smashed shops; scene of devastation described; city, almost entirely Albanian, has long been center of Albanian nationalism; discoveries confirm refugees' widely reported accounts; suspected mass graves are also discovered at sites of previously reported massacres in Velika Krusa and Bella Crkva; discovery 21 bodies in farmhouse in Mala Krusa discussed; photos; map (M)

1999-06-16.title: Muscovites Savor a Caper After Being Down So Long
1999-06-16.abstract: Unexpected seizure of airfield in Pristina, Kosovo, by 200 Russian soldiers, who have kept NATO forces at bay is insignificant military operation but powerful metaphor of Russia's disdain for West in general and US in particular; rare act of one-upmanship by faded power against military that once viewed Russia with fear and awe has given Russians moment to savor (M)

1999-06-16.title: Abu Ghosh Journal; His Pot of Gold Gives a Sparkle to the Whole Town
1999-06-16.abstract: Israeli Arab Jawdat Ibrahim, who won $17.5 million jackpot in Illinois State Lottery in 1990, returns to hometown of Abu Ghosh outside Jerusalem, where he is parlaying funds into peculiar role for himself; is using money to improve town's economy and his new-found influence to designate himself as emissary of peace and brotherhood; photo; map (M)

1999-06-16.title: Korean Fight At Sea Evolves Into Exchange Of Charges
1999-06-16.abstract: Confrontation between North Korean and South Korean naval vessels in disputed waters of Yellow Sea recedes into verbal clash, with North Korea demanding apology and lashing out at South for sinking one of its warships; each side blames other for firing first; confrontation began on June 7, when North Korean naval vessels escorted fishing boats past boundary in Yellow Sea separating both countries; incident is likely to put additional strains on North Korea's relations with South and with rest of world (M)

1999-06-17.title: KOSOVO UPDATE
1999-06-17.abstract: Update of developments in Kosvo, Serbia (S)

1999-06-17.title: China Arrests 2 Leaders of Farmers' Protest
1999-06-17.abstract: China arrests Yang Yaojin and Hu Zhiping, leaders of farmers' protests, five months after riot policemen violently dispersed demonstration by angry farmers in Hunan province (S)

1999-06-17.title: Cohen Meets With Russian; Progress Seen On Troop Issue
1999-06-17.abstract: Defense Sec William S Cohen and Russian counterpart, Marshal Igor D Sergeyev, reportedly make progress toward resolution of touchy issue of Russia's role in Kosovo after seven hours of intense talks in Helsinki; negotiators are under pressure to come up with agreement before Pres Clinton meets with Pres Boris Yeltsin in Cologne, Germany, in several days (M)

1999-06-17.title: With Armed Serbs Still on the Scene, Albanians in Kosovo's Capital Are Wary but Hopeful
1999-06-17.abstract: Mood in Pristina, capital of Serbia's Kosovo province, remains tentative, wary and restrained even as NATO-led peacekeepers patrol streets in jeeps, armored personnel carriers and sometimes on foot; some Serbian police and military personnel remain in city, and many are armed and angry; photo (M)

1999-06-17.title: World Briefing
1999-06-17.abstract: ASIA    INDIA: COURT CLEARS JAYALALITHA -- A former actress turned political power broker who helped topple the Government in April was acquitted of one of a number of corruption charges brought against her. A special court in the southern state of Tamil Nadu ruled that Jayalalitha Jayaram, 52, the state's chief minister from 1991-96, was innocent of charges that she received $1.5 million in a coal import scandal. She said the decision vindicated her, but she still faces other bribery charges.   Stephen Kinzer (NYT)

1999-06-18.title: Leaving Poland, John Paul Links the Country With Its Faith
1999-06-18.abstract: Pope John Paul II, bidding farewell to Poland, makes stop at his and his country's most venerated shrine, Black Madonna of Czestochowa, medieval icon of Virgin Mary that for Poles also carries vast patriotic significance (M)

1999-06-18.title: Negotiations On the Role Of Russia Intensify
1999-06-18.abstract: American and Russian negotiators return to the table in Helsinki with orders to work through the night, with key issue of distribution of Russian forces in Kosovo still unsettled (M)

1999-06-18.title: Clinton Sees No Quick Effect Of Indictment on Milosevic
1999-06-18.abstract: Pres Clinton, still trying to sort out the untidy aftermath of Kosovo war, suggests that indictment of Slobodan Milosevic by war crimes tribunal will serve to isolate Serbia on international stage but will not immediately affect Milosevic's personal position in Belgrade; photo of Clinton with French Pres Jacques Chirac outside Elysee Palace (M)

1999-06-18.title: The Pull of Home Outweighs The Dangers for Kosovars
1999-06-18.abstract: Aid workers at Kosovo border put up big signs warning returning refugees of dangers of mines; nothing, it appears, can dam flow of refugees; ignoring all dangers, more than 13,000 refugees squeeze through crowded crossing point at Vermitca, on the way, finally, home; photos (M)

1999-06-18.title: Screaming Lord Sutch, 58; Eccentric Even for England
1999-06-18.abstract: Screaming Lord Sutch, leader of Official Monster Raving Loony party, who delighted even the victims of his lampooning of British politics over three decades, dies in London, an apparent suicide, at age 58; photo (M)

1999-06-19.title: Accord Is Reached on Integrating Russian Troops in Kosovo Force
1999-06-19.abstract: Russia and United States sign military agreement that integrates 3,600 Russian troops into Kosovo peacekeeping force, dividing them among three of five sectors led by NATO countries; accord comes after three days of grueling negotiations, breaking tense stalemate over Russia's role in Kosovo; control of airport is to be shared: Russian commanders will be in charge of ground operations, while NATO will oversee air traffic control; Russian negotiators had originally demanded separate Russian sector, as had Pres Boris N Yeltsin; photo (M)

1999-06-19.title: Growing Signs That Serbs Removed Dead At Mass Graves
1999-06-19.abstract: Villagers in Izbica, identified as one of largest sites of mass burials in Kosovo, say Yugoslav forces spent two days unearthing bodies and trucking them off; tampering was on June 2 and 3, five days after Pres Slobodan Milosevic had been indicted for war crimes by war crimes tribunal for former Yugoslavia; one of seven mass executions cited in indictment is one that villagers say occurred in Izbica on Mar 28, taking lives of scores of civilians (M)

1999-06-19.title: Dingjiaergou Journal; Remains of a Day, Unearthed, Enrich Another
1999-06-19.abstract: Farmers of Dingjiaergou, poverty-stricken region of China, are digging up lucrative new crop--cache of nearly intact 15 million-year-old fossils, which they hope to sell; valuable specimens excavated in last two months include ancient crocodiles, rhinos and pigs; neither farmers nor local officials seem perturbed that their digging might be illegal; but many scientists are concerned that these amateur fossil hunters may be destroying valuable scientific evidence with their rudimentary picks and shovels; photos; map (M)

1999-06-19.title: In a Kosovo Town, an Experiment in Coexistence Ends After a Few Days
1999-06-19.abstract: Serbs are fleeing Orahovac, Kosovo, where they initally hoped to stay and try to live again beside Albanians; allege that guerrillas of Kosovo Liberation Army kidnapped seven men as they went house to house confiscating weapons; say guerrillas only came out of hiding since arrival of NATO peacekeepers, leading them to believe peacekeepers are cooperating with former rebels; Serbs have distrusted NATO peacekeepers from start, because it was NATO that bombed Serbs for more than two months, to some degree in cooperation with Albanian guerrillas on the ground; photo (M)

1999-06-19.title: KOSOVO UPDATE
1999-06-19.abstract: Update on latest events in Kosovo; photo (M)

1999-06-20.title: Upbeat Israeli Leader Has U.S. Guessing
1999-06-20.abstract: Israeli Prime Min-elect Ehud Barak has declined to discr

1999-06-20.title: Can She Run Indonesia? It's About Islam, or Is It?
1999-06-20.abstract: Indonesians, including Islamic religious leaders, are split on whether woman can be president, issue raised by some of Megawati Sukarnoputri's opponents; Islam, restive but largely quiescent political force for decades, has suddenly emerged as potentially divisive issue in vacuum of slow post-election vote count; photos (M)

1999-06-20.title: Kosovars to Get European Help For Rebuilding
1999-06-20.abstract: European Union says it will give $500 million annually for three years to rebuild Kosovo; seeks strategy to channel money to area without aiding Serbia controlled by Pres Slobodan Milosevic; holds he must be isolated; Samuel R Berger, national security adviser indicates that Russia blocked statement from summit conference of seven leading industrial nations and Russia that Serbia will get no aid while Milosevic is in power; Prime Min Sergei V Stepashin reportedly argues for aid for Serbia; photo (M)

1999-06-20.title: At Windsor, Royal Wedding Has a Common Touch
1999-06-20.abstract: Britain's Prince Edward weds Sophie Rhys-Jones; Queen Elizabeth names him Earl of Wessex and Viscount Severn; his wife becomes Countess of Wessex; photos (M)

1999-06-20.title: Serb Police Exit Joking, But Menace Lingers On
1999-06-20.abstract: Serbian special policemen withdrawing from Mitrovica, Kosovo, exit joking, but amid sense of lingering menace; ethnic Albanians on street give wide birth to building they used as base; Serbian civilians pack up to leave with police (M)

1999-06-21.title: U.S. AND RUSSIANS STRIVE  TO  REPAIR FRAYED RELATIONS
1999-06-21.abstract: Pres Clinton and Pres Boris N Yeltsin meet in Cologne, Germany, and reportedly to agree to put aside ill feelings generated between US and Russia by war in Kosovo; Clinton says the two countries are in position to have stronger relationship than before conflict started; leaders focused on Russian economy, Moscow's sharing of nuclear technology and arms control; issue joint statement agreeing for first time to begin discussions on changes to linchpin accord on nuclear weapons between two countries--something Clinton has desired; photo (M)

1999-06-21.title: After Hiding for Months, Albanian Is Local Hero
1999-06-21.abstract: Veton Surroi, a newspaper publisher and leader of Albanian community in Pristina, Kosovo, tells how he survived there through three months of turmoil, evading Serbian police squads and paramilitaries herding Albanians out of city and hunting down intellectuals; photo (M)

1999-06-21.title: Henri VI, 90, the Orleans Heir Apparent to the Throne of France
1999-06-21.abstract: Henri VI, Count of Paris, who might have been King if France were still monarchy, dies at age 90; photo (M)

1999-06-21.title: Last Serbian Troops Pull Out of Kosovo
1999-06-21.abstract: Last of estimated 40,000 Serbian forces leave Kosovo a few hours ahead of NATO's deadline for withdrawal, effectively ending Yugoslavia's control over province; NATO's Secretary General, Javier Solana, announces lifting of threat to resume bombing, formally ending 78-day air war that culminated with occupation of Kosovo by NATO peacekeepers; Kosovo Liberation Army command signs draft agreement to disband and turn over weapons within 30 days, but confrontations between NATO troops and KLA members continue; Serbian withdrawal is met with jubilation among Kosovo Albanians, as well as fresh acts of retribution even as 18,000 NATO troops move to exert semblance of control; Albanians who recently emerged from hiding loot and burn homes belonging to Serbs; violence has prompted new exodus of Serbs, more than 50,000 of whom have fled since NATO troops arrived; photos; map (M)

1999-06-21.title: From Siberia to Israeli Cabinet (No, He's Not Sharansky)
1999-06-21.abstract: Yuli Edelstein, Israel's Immigration Minister, is among new breed of Israeli politicians; they are younger, from greater diversity of ethnic backgrounds and less likely to be veterans of great Israeli wars, lifelong Zionists or even Jewish; Edelstein is former Soviet refusenik whose father is Russian Orthodox priest; is No. 2 to Natan Sharansky, who is at helm of small but powerful Russian immigrant party; photo (M)

1999-06-22.title: Kamal Eddin Hussein, 77, Figure In Coup Against Farouk in 1952
1999-06-22.abstract: Kamal Eddin Hussein, Egyptian army officer who helped to overthrow King Farouk of Egypt in 1952 and became Vice President of Egypt, dies at age 77; photo (M)

1999-06-22.title: NATO's Peacekeeping Force, Defied by Both Sides, Struggles to Keep Order
1999-06-22.abstract: Dozens of Serbs in civilian dress, who call themselves 'warrior citizens,' prevent ethnic Albanians for third consecutive day from entering predominantly Serbian sector of northern Kosovo industrial town of Mitrovica, jeering and threatening any who dared to enter; declare about one-third of city and predominantly Serbian towns to north 'Serbian zone' in direct challenge to NATO authority; NATO officials say present strategy is to avoid confronting Serbs in hope situation will calm over time; photo (M)

1999-06-22.title: World Briefing
1999-06-22.abstract: ASIA    INDIA, PAKISTAN: MORE FIGHTING -- Indian soldiers cleared Islamic guerrillas from another Himalayan outpost yesterday, a day after driving them from the highest peak overlooking India's northern highway in Kashmir, an Indian spokesman said. India has reported steady progress in pushing back the fighters, who it says infiltrated from Pakistan in an attempt to change the 1972 cease-fire line that divides the region. Western nations have been pressing the two nuclear powers to settle the dispute peacefully. (AP)    CAMBODIA: 2 CHARGED IN TOURISTS' KILLING -- Two former Khmer Rouge guerrillas who were later commissioned in the Cambodian Army, Sam Bith, top, and Chhouk Rin, have been formally charged in the killing of three backpackers in 1994, a state prosecutor said. The two were arraigned in Phnom Penh Municipal Court two weeks after another former guerrilla, Nuon Paet, was sentenced to life in prison for ordering the killing of the three tourists, David Wilson of Australia, Mark Slater of Britain and Jean-Michel Braquet of France. The three, all in their mid-20s, were abducted from a train on July 26, 1994. (AP)

1999-06-22.title: U.N. Committee, Under Pressure, Limits Rights Groups
1999-06-22.abstract: UN Committee for Nongovernmental Organizations, led by small group of developing nations, inflicts major setbacks on human rights groups that seek to expose and combat political oppression; withdraws accreditation of Zurich-based Christian Solidarity International and refuses accreditation, amid lobbying by China, to Human Rights in China, New York organization that monitors abuses in China; reverses occur as enforcement of norms of human rights seems to be growing, following 1998 decision to establish international criminal court and tribunals to judge those responsible for genocide; group of organizations led by Human Rights Watch of New York plan new campaign to bring more exiled dictators and other leaders accused of human rights abuses to justice in their own countries or those that extend asylum (M)

1999-06-22.title: Indonesia's Vote: Reformers May Be Disappointed
1999-06-22.abstract: News analysis: will of Indonesian people may in large part be denied, regardless of outcome of vote count in recent Parliamentary election; country's complex electoral system does not guarantee victory for challengers, even those who win most votes; none of current candidates are reformers at heart even though reform has been watchword of election campaign (M)

1999-06-23.title: Top General Would Speed Deployment For Kosovo
1999-06-23.abstract: NATO Gen Wesley K Clark, visiting Macedonia with Pres Clinton, calls on NATO allies to move quickly to flood Kosovo with peacekeepers as acts of violence and retribution continue in province; suggests that more than 50,000 peacekeepers orginally planned might be needed; some Army forces under his own command apparently may be slow in leaving Germany for Balkans; NATO troops have become increasingly visible in Kosovo's major cities, but are seen only sporatically in smaller, remote villages racked by tensions (M)

1999-06-23.title: Austerity Plan to Test Schroeder's Government
1999-06-23.abstract: FRANKFURT—  Chancellor Gerhard Schroeder is due to announce Wednesday an unpopular austerity plan that political analysts view as the last chance for his shaky coalition government to revive Europe's biggest economy.

1999-06-23.title: In Wake of Espionage, Debate On New Nuclear Arms Agency
1999-06-23.abstract: Warren Rudman, who heads Pres Clinton's foreign intelligence advisory board, tells unusual joint hearing of four Senate committees that blame for lax security at weapons labs that enabled China to acquire nuclear weapons design secrets is widespread and bipartisan and requires independent solution; Energy Sec Bill Richardson opposes creating semi-autonomous agency within Energy Department to oversee nuclear arms programs; instead backs creation of department undersecretary to take control of programs; Richardson and Rudman later testify before House Commerce Committee (M)

1999-06-23.title: More Countries Studying Year 2000 Disruptions, U.N. Panel Finds
1999-06-23.abstract: Organizers of United Nations meeting on year 2000 computer problems say disruptions are unlikely to be as widespread or serious as earlier feared; Amb Ahmad Kamal, who heads UN working group on information technology, says corrective action is under way everywhere; delegates from 173 nations attended meeting, up from 120 at first meeting in Dec; organizers say regional sessions that led up to meeting could spur international cooperation on technical issues long after year 2000 challenge has passed (M)

1999-06-23.title: Chile Leader Moves to Aid His Economy, And Prestige
1999-06-23.abstract: Pres Eduardo Frei of Chile overhauls Cabinet and sets economic stimulus package; responds to rising unemployment and increasingly tense relations with military upset over Government's inability to bring home Gen Augusto Pinochet, former dictator under house arrest in Britain; unexpected moves are aimed at boosting Government's declining credibility and aiding presidential campaign of Christian Democrat-Socialist coalition candidate, Ricardo Lagos (M)

1999-06-24.title: As Yen Weakens, Japan's Neighbors Begin to Worry
1999-06-24.abstract: HONG KONG—  As investors stampeded out of Asia in 1997, igniting a global financial crisis after Thailand floated its currency, many analysts concluded that the world had been too sanguine about foreign-exchange risk and its potential to wreak havoc on capital markets and national economies.

1999-06-24.title: Even in Towns NATO Hit, Albanians Fault Serbs
1999-06-24.abstract: Planje, Kosovo, was more or less leveled in near-daily attacks by NATO planes in weeks before peace agreement was signed with Serbia; residents who are returning to village say they blame Serbia for destruction, not NATO; photo (M)

1999-06-24.title: In Arms Sales, Japan Coddles Its Own
1999-06-24.abstract: Japan's $16 billion defense industry in theory has been partly opened to foreign competition, but analysts say odds are heavily stacked in favor of local companies; they say that couple of contracts can mean life or death of weapons division of Japanese company in these hard economic times (M)

1999-06-24.title: C.I.A. Analyst Questioned Target Before Chinese Embassy Bombing
1999-06-24.abstract: Government officials say middle-level analyst in Central Intelligence Agency raised doubts about whether military planners were zeroing in on right building shortly before US bombs struck Chinese Embassy in Belgrade on May 7; Clinton Administration begins to brief Congress about internal investigation of bombing; briefing includes account of analyst's warning; Administration recently provided similar reconstruction to Chinese officials; strike, which killed three Chinese and wounded at least 20 others, was serious setback to relations with China (M)

1999-06-24.title: Serb Is Killed In Gun Battle With Marines
1999-06-24.abstract: US officials say local Serbs in civilian clothes opened fire on checkpoint run by US marines in Zegra, Kosovo, provoking firefight that left one Serb dead and two others wounded; say fourth Serb was arrested after marines stormed house where Serbs had retreated; say no marines were wounded; gunfire underscores volatility of NATO's operation in Kosovo, 12 days after peacekeepers first arrived; it is second time that US troops have come under direct fire; photo (M)

1999-06-25.title: Leaders of Kosovo Rebels Tied to Deadly Power Play
1999-06-25.abstract: Current and former commanders in Kosovo Libveration Army, and some Western diplomats, say that senior commanders of rebel army, which signed disarmament agreement with NATO, carried out assassinations, arrests and purges within their ranks to thwart potential rivals; say that campaign, in which as many as half dozen top rebel commanders were shot dead, was directed by Hashim Thaci and two of his lieutenants, Azem Syla and Xhavit Haliti; Thaci denies, through spokesman, that he was responsible for any such killings; photo (L)

1999-06-25.title: U.S. Move Has Hong Kong Worried It Will Lose Trade
1999-06-25.abstract: Fears about Hong Kong's future as an Asian trading post grow since United States Senate moved to tighten controls on exports to Hong Kong in response to a Congressional report on military espionage and China; Senate approves legislation that would impose more stringent controls on export of equipment and technology with military uses to Hong Kong and Macao unless both territories allow United States to verify that sensitive shipments end up in right hands (M)

1999-06-25.title: Serbs Flee While Albanians Loot, And Marines Find Peace Is Tricky
1999-06-25.abstract: Some villages across Kosovo are gripped by new contortion of fear, violence and retribution, following United States marines' firefight with Serbs that kills one Serb and wounds two others; virtually every Serb in village of Zegra, Kosovo, leaves, most in single, bedraggled column of cars, trucks and trailers, some pulled by tractors, others by oxen or horses; as they leave, ignoring entreaties of their Orthodox priest to stay, smoke and flames pour from at least a dozen houses (L)

1999-06-25.title: Student Strike in Capital Jarring All of Mexico
1999-06-25.abstract: Protest leaders at National Autonomous University, Mexico's biggest university, seek more power for students and oppose Mexico's shift away from welfare state; student strike began on April 21 as protest against plan by administration to make those who could afford it pay tuition; university's president, Francisco Barnes de Castro, admitted defeat and agreed to make all tuition payments voluntary after seven weeks; but student strikers were not satisfied, and strike, which closed down classrooms for 270,000 students, is now in its third month and shows no sign of moving towards resolution; photos (L)

1999-06-26.title: Lebanon And Israel Still Tense After Clash
1999-06-26.abstract: Tense calm prevails along border between Israel and Lebanon after Israel's most severe bombardment of Lebanon since 1996; civilians on both sides wait for next rocket or bomb to fall; photos; map (M)

1999-06-26.title: Berlin Holocaust Memorial Approved
1999-06-26.abstract: German Parliament approves plan for building of vast memorial in heart of Berlin to six million Jewish victims of Holocaust; vote opens way for construction near Brandenburg Gate of project designed by New York architect Peter Eisenman; it will combine field of more than 2,000 stone pillars with building that is officially being called 'documentation center' but will have many of attributes of a Holocaust museum; photo of model of design (M)

1999-06-26.title: BBC Chooses Ally of Blair As Director, To Tories' Ire
1999-06-26.abstract: British Broadcasting Corp stirs passionate debate by appointing Greg Dyke, head of Pearson Television, as its next director general; Conservative opposition questions BBC's independence, and many fear a 'dumbing down' of BBC programs in view of Pearson's association with shows like Baywatch and a puppet called Roland Rat; appointment focuses attention anew on $87,000 that Dyke has donated in recent years to political campaigns of Prime Min Tony Blair and his followers (M)

1999-06-26.title: Israel Moves To Coalition, And Changes Are in Offing
1999-06-26.abstract: Natan Sharansky, Russian immigrant leader, will be next Interior Minister in Israel, wresting control of powerful ministry from ultra-Orthodox Shas Party, which has held it for years; Sharansky, whose party campaigned for Interior Ministry, signs agreement to join coalition Government of Prime Min-elect Ehud Barak (S)

1999-06-26.title: World Briefing
1999-06-26.abstract: Russia moves step closer toward securing new $4.5 billion loan from IMF when Communist-dominated Parliament passes several laws to raise Government revenue and restructure collapsed banking sector; Russia has not done everything IMF has demanded (S)

1999-06-27.title: In the Rubble That Was Kosovo, Damage Tally Has Barely Begun
1999-06-27.abstract: Vast destruction by Serbs of ethnic Albanian property in Kosovo has barely begun to be tallied; costs of physical reconstruction are unknown; hopes of progress in impoverished region are set back years, if not decades; destruction, mostly confined to rural areas before Mar 24 start of NATO bombing, shifted to cities and intensified after bombing as Serbs apparently sought revenge and to drive Albanians away for good; map; photo (M)

1999-06-27.title: When U.S. Learned Of China Spy Charge
1999-06-27.abstract: Senior White House officials were informed that China might have stolen American nuclear secrets in 1995, nearly a year earlier than the Clinton Administration originally disclosed, officials now say.

1999-06-27.title: Live, in Belgrade, the Milosevic News
1999-06-27.abstract: State-controlled television that Slobodan Milosevic has used so masterfullly for last decade is now portraying a tranquil Kosovo, promoting Serbian defeat as victory and focusing on reconstruction, leaving millions of Serbs in ignorance or confused denial; but as pace of misleading information accelerates, there are indications that credibility of Milosevic's programming is beginning to slide, particularly as it becomes more difficult for government to hide both its lack of money and the flood of refugees who have fled north from Kosovo; photo (Belgrade Journal) (M)

1999-06-27.title: U.S. Tries to Punish Aide Over U.N. Leaks
1999-06-27.abstract: Linda S Shenwick, administrative and budget officer at United States Mission to United Nations, is accused of 'insubordination' and threatened with immediate transfer; State Department officials say Shenwick funneled damaging information to Congressional Republicans and reporters in effort to sabotage Clinton Administration effort to pay $1 billion in back dues to UN; her lawyer, Victoria Toensing, says Shenwick is classic whistle-blower who is being punished for exposing waste and fraud (M)

1999-06-27.title: Peacekeepers, at Last, Reopen Pristina Airport
1999-06-27.abstract: NATO peacekeepers reopen airport in Pristina, Kosovo, clearing way for regular flights of troops, supplies, and, eventually, civilians; first military aircraft, a Russian cargo plane, lands; photo (M)

1999-06-28.title: Tough Year Is Projected For Farms Of Russia
1999-06-28.abstract: Russian and American officials say blisteringly hot summer and reverberations from Russia's economic crisis are taking devastating toll on nation's crops, making it likely that Moscow will appeal for American food aid (M)

1999-06-28.title: Activists Thrust Agendas Into Post-Suharto Void
1999-06-28.abstract: Hundreds of nongovernmental organizations in Indonesia, notably Urban Poor Consortium led by Wardah Hafidz, have been boldly pushing their agendas since forced resignation of Pres Suharto in 1998; goals include human rights, environmental protection, workers' protection, legal aid and political freedoms as well as rights for poor; are one building block of new civil society, part of transformation of Indonesia that is realigning patters of power; photo (M)

1999-06-28.title: Morocco's King Loosens His Grip, and Holds On
1999-06-28.abstract: King Hassan II, who has ruled Morocco despotically for past 38 years, is recasting image as author of 'Hassanian democracy,' hybrid that has widened political freedoms while retaining paramount political power of Alawite dynasty that he heads; has established elected lower house of Parliament, in which leftist parties gained largest bloc of seats in elections in 1998; has ordered cleanup of country's human rights record to include release of dozens of political prisoners and payments of $500-per-month compensation to families of prisoners who disappeared or died; has retained curbs on hard-line Islamists, who are forbidden by King to form political parties or to contest elections; while palace officials acknowledge his absence from public view is because of ill health, they deny suggestions from Western diplomats that Hassan is seriously ill; photo; map (L)

1999-06-28.title: In Ulster, Hints of a Shift to Save Peace Effort
1999-06-28.abstract: David Trimble, First Minister of Northern Ireland Assembly, says stalled peace effort in Ulster can be saved by simple statement from Sinn Fein, political wing of the Irish Republican Army, that IRA is committed to disarming by May 2000; photo (M)

1999-06-28.title: Croatia's President Polishes a National Myth
1999-06-28.abstract: Discontent is growing in Croatia under Pres Franjo Tudjman, whose virulent nationalism and penchant for domination of ethnically pure state is exceeded in Balkans only by Slobodan Milosevic in Yugoslavia; his efforts to radiate new Croatian glory, at time when economy is near collapse, has met with public outrage; opinion polls suggest his ruling Christian Democratic Union will be trounced by opposition by margin of 2-to-1 in parliamentary elections later in 1999; map; photo (M)

1999-06-29.title: Likud Decides Not to Join A Coalition Led by Barak
1999-06-29.abstract: Israel's rightist Likud Party withdraws from coalition talks with Prime Min-elect Ehud Barak, announcing that it will not join his government and is headed for opposition; move defines contours of Barak's emerging coalition as he negotiates with Shas, ultra-Orthodox party of Sephardic Jews that is third-largest faction in Parliament and likely partner in new Govt; Barak has already reached agreements with three parties and accord with Shas would give Barak 77-seat majority in 120-seat Parliament; Shas relatively dovish positions on peace talks and territorial compromise with palestinbians contrasts with Likud's harder line (M)

1999-06-29.title: Mixed Results at Congo Cease-Fire Talks
1999-06-29.abstract: Negotiations to end 11-month-old war in Congo inch forward as foreign and defense ministers of all the nations involved in conflict meet in Zambia to discuss draft text for cease-fire; talks, already days behind, break up within two hours; Congo and Zimbabwe, one of its allies, reportedly objected to parts of document, which was hammered out by lower level officials (M)

1999-06-29.title: In a Wave of Balkan Charity Comes Drug Aid of Little Use
1999-06-29.abstract: World Health Organization says approximately one-third to half aid provided by corporate America and others for more than million of ethnic Albanian refugees who feld Kosovo, Serbia, is inappropriate and likely to gather dust in warehouses or be destroyed at government expense; critics from international relief agencies say problem arises because there are far too many incentives for companies to empty out warehouses, regardless of need, and for charities to ferry it along; are angered over what they regard as attitude, especially prevalent among American donors, that any gift is better than none (M)

1999-06-29.title: Beijing Journal; Group's Morning Exercises Are Politically Suspect
1999-06-29.abstract: Chinese Govt is worried over Falun Gong, or Buddhist Law, organization that melds Buddhism, meditation, Chinese exercise techniques and mystical ideas of exiled founder, Li Hongzhi, into wildly popular movement; has been involved in subtle battle of words and wills with group since 10,000 members unexpectedly staged illegal sit-in around Beijing's leadership compound, demanding official tolerance and recognition; claiming tens of millions of ardent folowers in China, Buddhist Law has emerged as powerful if hard-to-define force to be reckoned with; group has no obvious political agenda and claims only loose organization, but demonstration in April displayed enormous power to move its members aned ability to embarrass Communist Party if it does not get its way; photos (M)

1999-06-29.title: Panel Criticizes Extent of Arafat's Power
1999-06-29.abstract: International study, sponsored by Council on Foreign Relations in New York and paid for by European Commission and Govt of Norway, criticizes Yasir Arafat's concentration of executive power, while expressing compassion for Palestinian Authority; says Palestine Liberation Organization president has concentrated power so effectively that he personally approves all senior officials' vacations and their per diem expenses; says he has used Govt to ease unemployment and to reward political loyalty to such extent that public sector is bloated, inefficient and risks incurring staggering debts to pay expenses; says Palestine Legislative Council is relatively impotent, judiciary is in disarray, unregulated monopolies rule many industries and police force abuses its powers; its other findings and recommendations discussed (M)

1999-06-30.title: Low-Key Luxury Is In, and Italy's Fabric Maestros Can Supply It
1999-06-30.abstract: MILAN—  Today's well-dressed man with money to burn can afford to look flash, but the fashion statement he prefers to project is the look of low-key luxury.

1999-06-30.title: Karekin I, Armenian Patriarch, Dies at 66
1999-06-30.abstract: Catholicos Karekin I, spiritual leader of Armenian Orthodox Christians worldwide, dies at age 66; photo (M)

1999-06-30.title: Nazi Victims Get Details on Compensation Claims
1999-06-30.abstract: Advertising drive ordered by United States court opens to inform victims of Nazi persecution how to claim compensation from $1.25 billion fund set up by Union Bank of Switzerland and Credit Suisse; people can apply even if they or relatives never had Swiss account; World Jewish Congress and panel of lawyers oversee effort (M)

1999-06-30.title: Phnom Penh Journal; Coming Khmer Rouge Trial Rouses Jail's Ghosts
1999-06-30.abstract: Scene at former Tuol Sleng prison, Phnom Penh, Cambodia, described; prison, preserved unrestored as museum since Khmer Rouge fled in 1979, was used as torture chamber by Duch (Kang Kek Ieu), who now awaits trial; photo (M)

1999-06-30.title: Robbers Hit Zedillo Presidential Guard, Kill 2 and Steal Payroll
1999-06-30.abstract: Armed robbers ambush officers of elite Presidential Guard near Pres Ernesto Zedillo's residence, Mexico City, Mex, stealing $50,000 military payroll, killing two soldiers and wounding two; brazen midday attack seems certain to spur view that crime, key issue in state and national elections, is out of control (M)
